front: Poem: 'among twenty snowy mountains, the only moving thing was the eye' of the title creature; asks 'thin men of Haddam' about golden birds
back: Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird (Wallace Stevens)
work: Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird

front: Poem: commands 'Call the roller of big cigars' to 'whip in kitchen cups concupiscent curds' at a funeral where the corpse's 'horny feet protrude'
Poem: a woman stays home in her 'peignoir' enjoying 'coffee and oranges in a sunny chair'; declares 'Death is the mother of beauty'
Poem: a jar placed on a hill in Tennessee 'took dominion everywhere' and 'did not give of bird or bush'; the wilderness is called 'slovenly'
Poem: dedicated to Henry Church; declares the supreme fiction 'must be abstract,' 'must change,' and 'must give pleasure'; opens 'Begin, ephebe'
back: Notes Toward a Supreme Fiction (Wallace Stevens)

front: Poem: 'Music is feeling, then, not sound'; named for a Shakespearean 'rude mechanical'; retells Susanna and the Elders with 'Byzantines' making 'a noise like tambourines'
back: Peter Quince at the Clavier (Wallace Stevens)

front: Poem: a woman who 'sang beyond the genius of the sea' is 'the single artificer of the world'; speaker calls out to 'Ramon Fernandez'
back: The Idea of Order at Key West (Wallace Stevens)
work: The Idea of Order at Key West

front: Poem: houses 'haunted by white night-gowns'; 'People are not going to dream of baboons and periwinkles'; a drunk sailor catches 'tigers in red weather'
Poem: 'One must have a mind of winter' to regard frost and pines; ends with 'the nothing that is not there and the nothing that is'
Poem: contrasts 'the beauty of inflections' with 'the beauty of innuendos'; states 'A man and a woman / Are one'
back: Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird (Wallace Stevens)
work: Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird

front: Poem: a 'fire-fangled' bird sings 'without human feeling' in a palm tree 'at the end of the mind'
Author: Helen Vendler's On Extended Wings is a major study of this poet's longer poems; she called 'Earthy Anecdote' a 'manifesto'
